skill.name: azure-ai-translation-text-py
skill.description: Guides Python developers using the Azure AI Text Translation SDK for translation, transliteration, language detection, dictionary lookup, and sentence boundary operations.
skill.summary: Use Azure AI Text Translation from Python with credential, translation, dictionary, and language examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution alerts, but review found they are markdown code fences and inline method names in SDK documentation, not executable skill logic. The network URL and environment access are legitimate Azure Translator configuration examples, and the weak cryptography alerts are keyword false positives. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown code fences and inline method names used to document Azure SDK examples. The skill contains no script body that executes these snippets automatically, so the command execution findings are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible markdown fences, sample Python blocks, or table inline code. I found no executable wrapper, install hook, or instruction that runs arbitrary commands.

low_findings.title: Azure Credential Environment Variables Are Documented Configuration
low_findings.description: The skill shows Python examples that read Azure Translator key, region, and endpoint values from environment variables. This is expected for Azure SDK authentication, but users should still avoid exposing these values in logs or shared prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables are used directly to initialize Azure SDK clients. This is legitimate configuration access, with only normal credential handling risk.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Translator Network Endpoint
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a placeholder Azure Cognitive Services endpoint for a user-owned Translator resource. It does not point to an unknown third party and is part of the documented service configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint uses an Azure Cognitive Services host template and is presented as a configuration placeholder. I found no evidence that data is sent to an unrelated destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings occur on descriptive markdown lines, not cryptographic code. No hash, cipher, signing, or encryption implementation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter or table headers. There is no cryptographic API usage or algorithm selection at those locations.

content.user_title: Translate Text with Azure AI in Python
content.value_statement: Python teams need clear examples for Azure text translation without searching across SDK docs. This skill provides focused prompts and patterns for translation, transliteration, language detection, and dictionary lookup.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the azure-ai-translation-text Python package.
Explains API key, region, custom endpoint, and Entra ID authentication patterns.
Provides examples for translating one text or batches into one or more languages.
Covers transliteration, dictionary lookup, dictionary examples, and supported language discovery.
Includes synchronous and asynchronous TextTranslationClient usage patterns.
Documents translation options for HTML input, profanity handling, alignment, and sentence lengths.
limitations: Requires an active Azure Translator resource and valid credentials.
Does not create Azure resources or manage billing configuration.
Does not guarantee translation quality for every language pair or domain.
Examples may need adjustment for SDK version changes and application error handling.

use_cases.title: Add Product Localization
use_cases.description: Translate user interface strings or help text from Python services into multiple target languages.

use_cases.title: Build Translation Workflows
use_cases.description: Create batch translation scripts that use Azure credentials, source language hints, and asynchronous calls.

use_cases.title: Explore Language Features
use_cases.description: Use dictionary lookup, transliteration, and supported language data to evaluate language support.

prompt_templates.title: Translate One Phrase
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the simplest Python example for translating one phrase to Spanish with Azure AI Text Translation.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Credentials
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure TextTranslationClient with environment variables for key, region, and endpoint.

prompt_templates.title: Translate a Batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python pattern for translating a batch of strings into French, German, and Japanese with error handling notes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Localization Utility
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an async Python localization helper using TextTranslationClient, source language hints, caching for supported languages, and safe credential handling.

output_examples.input: Translate two English onboarding messages into Spanish and French.
output_examples.output: A concise Python workflow that initializes TextTranslationClient, sends both messages in one request, and reads each returned language result.

output_examples.input: Find Spanish dictionary entries for the English word fly.
output_examples.output: A dictionary lookup explanation showing alternate translations, part of speech details, and confidence values for each result.

output_examples.input: Convert romaji text into Japanese script.
output_examples.output: A transliteration example that identifies the source language, source script, target script, and returned converted text.

best_practices: Store Azure credentials in environment variables or managed identity, not source files.
Batch related translation requests to reduce overhead and improve throughput.
Specify the source language when known to improve consistency and reduce detection errors.
anti_patterns: Do not paste Azure keys into prompts, logs, tickets, or committed files.
Do not send sensitive personal data to translation services without an approved data policy.
Do not translate HTML as plain text when markup structure must be preserved.

faq.question: Does this skill call Azure directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Your generated code calls Azure only when you run it with valid credentials.

faq.question: Which Python package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers the azure-ai-translation-text package and the TextTranslationClient API.

faq.question: Can it use Microsoft Entra ID?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a TextTranslationClient pattern that uses DefaultAzureCredential with an endpoint.

faq.question: Can it translate into multiple languages at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show one request with several target language codes.

faq.question: Does it include dictionary and transliteration features?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers dictionary entries, dictionary examples, transliteration, and supported language discovery.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Protect Azure credentials and review whether text sent for translation may contain sensitive data.
